agent_output:
# Domain Analysis: Smart Route Optimization

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This feature encodes the **last-mile delivery orchestration process** — the critical handoff between warehouse/hub operations and the customer's doorstep. It spans the moment a driver begins their shift through the completion (or failure) of each delivery attempt. This is not merely a routing algorithm; it is a **real-time dispatch and fulfillment coordination system** that must respect vehicle regulations, driver capabilities, operational constraints, and customer communication commitments.

### Bounded Contexts Identified

This feature touches **four distinct bounded contexts**. Conflating them is the single most common architectural mistake in logistics platforms.

| Bounded Context | Responsibility | Owner |
|---|---|---|
| **Route Planning** | Calculates and recalculates optimized stop sequences | Planning/Optimization Engine |
| **Fleet & Driver Management** | Driver shifts, vehicle assignments, licensing, vehicle classes | Fleet Operations |
| **Delivery Fulfillment** | Individual delivery lifecycle (pending → in-transit → delivered/failed) | Fulfillment/Dispatch |
| **Customer Notification** | Customer-facing communication, delivery windows, ETAs | Customer Experience |

### Ubiquitous Language

- **Zone**: A geographic region to which drivers and deliveries are assigned. Zones are the unit of dispatch — a driver works within exactly one zone per shift.
- **Shift**: A bounded work period during which a driver is active and assignable. A shift has a start time, an end time, and exactly one associated zone.
- **Optimized Route**: An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated to minimize total travel time/distance within a zone for a given driver's current shift. It is NOT a suggestion — it is the system's directive, though drivers may deviate.
- **Delivery Stop**: A single location where one or more deliveries must be attempted. Multiple parcels to the same address constitute ONE stop.
- **Vehicle Class**: A regulatory and physical classification of a vehicle (e.g., motorcycle, van, small truck, freight/HGV). Each class defines what categories of shipment it can legally and physically carry.
- **Licensed Vehicle Class**: The set of vehicle classes a driver is authorized to operate, determined by their license type and any company-specific certifications.
- **Stop Time**: The estimated duration a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes; overridable per zone by operations.
- **Next-3-Stops Window**: The subset of the optimized route consisting of the immediately upcoming three stops. Entering this window triggers customer notification. This is a **commitment to the customer** — not merely informational.
- **Route Deviation**: When a driver's actual GPS position or stop sequence diverges from the optimized route beyond a defined threshold. This is a **trigger event**, not merely a data point.
- **Failed Delivery**: A delivery attempt that did not result in successful handoff. Triggers route recalculation because the stop is either retried later or removed.
- **Recalculation Trigger**: An event that invalidates the current optimized route and forces re-computation. The spec defines exactly three: new delivery added, delivery marked failed, driver deviation.

---

## Business Rules

### Route Planning Context

| # | Rule | Invariant | Testable Assertion |
|---|---|---|---|
| RP-1 | A route is calculated automatically when a driver starts their shift | No (trigger rule) | Given a driver starts a shift in Zone A with 5 pending deliveries, when the shift begins, then an optimized route with those 5 stops is generated within [SLA] seconds |
| RP-2 | A route MUST be recalculated when a new delivery is added to the driver's zone | **Yes** | Given a driver has an active route in Zone A, when a new delivery is added to Zone A, then the route is recalculated to include the new delivery |
| RP-3 | A route MUST be recalculated when a delivery is marked as failed | **Yes** | Given a driver has an active route containing Delivery X, when Delivery X is marked failed, then the route is recalculated excluding that stop (or repositioning retry) |
| RP-4 | A route MUST be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route | **Yes** | Given a driver has an active route, when their actual position/sequence deviates beyond threshold, then the route is recalculated from their current position |
| RP-5 | Stop time defaults to 4 minutes per stop | No (soft default) | Given no zone-level override exists, when ETA is calculated, then each stop adds exactly 4 minutes to cumulative time |
| RP-6 | Operations team can override stop time per zone | No (configuration) | Given ops sets Zone A stop time to 6 minutes, when routes in Zone A are calculated, then each stop uses 6 minutes |

### Fleet & Driver Management Context

| # | Rule | Invariant | Testable Assertion |
|---|---|---|---|
| FD-1 | A driver MUST NOT be assigned a delivery that exceeds their licensed vehicle class | **Yes — HARD INVARIANT** | Given a driver is licensed for motorcycle only, when a freight shipment exists in their zone, then that shipment is NEVER included in their route |
| FD-2 | A driver operates within exactly one zone per shift | **Yes** | Given a driver starts a shift in Zone A, then they receive ONLY deliveries assigned to Zone A |
| FD-3 | Vehicle class compatibility is determined by the vehicle currently assigned to the driver's shift, not just the driver's license | **Yes** | Given a driver licensed for van and motorcycle is currently on a motorcycle shift, then they receive only motorcycle-compatible deliveries |

### Delivery Fulfillment Context

| # | Rule | Invariant | Testable Assertion |
|---|---|---|---|
| DF-1 | A delivery can only be in one active route at a time | **Yes** | Given Delivery X is on Driver A's route, then Delivery X MUST NOT appear on Driver B's route simultaneously |
| DF-2 | Only deliveries with status "pending" are eligible for route assignment | **Yes** | Given Delivery X has status "delivered" or "cancelled", then it is never included in any route calculation |
| DF-3 | A failed delivery changes state but does not disappear — it must be dispositioned | **Yes** | Given Delivery X is marked failed, then it transitions to a "failed" state requiring either retry scheduling or escalation — it is NOT silently dropped |

### Customer Notification Context

| # | Rule | Invariant | Testable Assertion |
|---|---|---|---|
| CN-1 | Customer is notified when their delivery enters the next-3-stops window | **Yes** | Given a customer's delivery is stop #4, when the driver completes a stop making it #3, then the customer receives a notification |
| CN-2 | A customer MUST NOT receive a "next 3 stops" notification more than once per delivery attempt | No (soft — but critical UX) | Given a customer was already notified for this attempt, when a recalculation moves them back into position 3, then no duplicate notification is sent |
| CN-3 | If a recalculation moves a delivery OUT of the next-3-stops window after notification was sent, the customer should be informed of the delay | No (soft — but high trust impact) | Given customer was notified they're in the window, when recalculation moves them to stop #5, then a "delay" notification is sent |

---

## Business Impact

**Financial:**
- Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, driver hours, and vehicle wear — typically 15-30% efficiency gain over manual dispatch. Stop time accuracy directly affects delivery density per shift (a 2-minute error × 30 stops = 1 hour of drift). Incorrect vehicle class assignment could result in regulatory fines, failed deliveries, and wasted trips.

**Operational:**
- Recalculation triggers create system load spikes. In dense urban zones, new deliveries may arrive every few minutes, causing continuous recalculation. The system must handle recalculation debouncing/throttling without violating the invariant. Zone-level stop time overrides are an operational lever — getting this wrong cascades into missed ETAs across the entire zone.

**User (Driver):**
- Drivers must trust the system. If routes are erratic (constant recalculation), drivers will ignore suggestions and revert to manual routing, defeating the purpose. Deviation detection must distinguish between intentional shortcuts (driver local knowledge) and genuine deviations.

**User (Customer):**
- The "next 3 stops" notification creates a **promise**. Once sent, the customer alters their behavior (goes home, waits at door). Breaking this promise — through recalculation-induced reordering — is a major trust violation. This is not a nice-to-have; it is a customer commitment.

**Risk:**
- **Regulatory risk**: Vehicle class violations are not just business rules — they are legal requirements. A motorcycle delivering freight could mean insurance invalidation. This must be enforced at the domain level, never delegated to UI validation alone.
- **Cascading failure risk**: A failing route optimization engine means no driver gets assigned deliveries. This needs graceful degradation (fall back to manual dispatch).
- **Data consistency risk**: Race conditions between "new delivery added" and "route recalculation in progress" could cause lost deliveries or double-assignments.

**Stakeholders Affected:**

| Group | Impact |
|---|---|
| **Drivers** | Primary workflow change — shift from manual to system-directed routing |
| **Customers** | Receive proactive notifications; expectations are now set by the system |
| **Operations Team** | Gains zone-level tuning controls; responsible for stop time accuracy |
| **Dispatch/Planning** | Largely automated out of manual route building; shifts to exception handling |
| **Compliance/Legal** | Vehicle class enforcement becomes system-enforced vs. manual checks |
| **Finance** | Measurable efficiency gains; fuel and labor cost reduction |

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### Core Flow

| # | Given | When | Then |
|---|---|---|---|
| AC-1 | A driver with a valid shift and 10 pending deliveries in their zone | The driver starts their shift | An optimized route containing all 10 eligible deliveries is generated and presented |
| AC-2 | A driver has an active route with 8 remaining stops | A new delivery is added to their zone | The route is recalculated to include the new delivery in an optimal position |
| AC-3 | A driver has an active route | Delivery #4 is marked as failed | The route is recalculated with 1 fewer stop, re-optimizing remaining sequence |
| AC-4 | A driver has an active route proceeding A→B→C→D | The driver skips B and drives to C | Deviation is detected and the route is recalculated from C's position |
| AC-5 | A customer's delivery is currently stop #5 | The driver completes stop #2 (delivery becomes stop #3) | The customer receives exactly one notification that their delivery is approaching |
| AC-6 | A driver is on a motorcycle | A freight shipment is pending in their zone | The freight shipment is excluded from the driver's route — no exceptions |
| AC-7 | Zone A has a stop time override of 6 minutes | A route is calculated for Zone A | All ETA calculations use 6 minutes per stop, not the 4-minute default |

### Edge Cases

| Scenario | Expected Behavior |
|---|---|
| Driver starts shift with zero pending deliveries in zone | Empty route is created; driver is placed in "awaiting deliveries" state; route auto-generates when first delivery arrives |
| Two drivers active in the same zone simultaneously | Deliveries are partitioned — each delivery appears on exactly one route. System must have an allocation strategy (e.g., proximity, load balancing) |
| Rapid-fire recalculation (3 new deliveries in 30 seconds) | System debounces/batches recalculations — does NOT fire 3 sequential recalculations. Final route includes all 3 new deliveries |
| Customer notified "next 3 stops" but recalculation pushes them to stop #6 | Customer receives a revised ETA / delay notification. The original promise is acknowledged as broken, not silently ignored |
| Driver's vehicle breaks down mid-shift | All remaining deliveries on this route must be unassigned and returned to the pending pool for reassignment to other drivers |
| A delivery is cancelled while on an active route | Route is recalculated without the cancelled delivery; if customer was already notified, a cancellation notification is sent |
| All deliveries in zone require freight but only motorcycle drivers are available | No routes are generated for motorcycle drivers; deliveries remain pending; operations is alerted to a capacity mismatch |
| Stop time override is set to 0 minutes | System rejects — stop time must be > 0. A delivery always takes nonzero time |
| Driver completes deliveries faster than 4-minute estimate | Route recalculation should use actual progress, not just estimated times, for remaining ETA accuracy |

---

## Confidence Assessment

```
confidence:
  level: 85
  high_confidence_areas:
    - Vehicle class invariant is non-negotiable and legally mandated
    - Recalculation triggers are clearly specified (3 explicit triggers)
    - Stop time default and override mechanism is well-defined
    - Customer notification window semantics (next 3 stops)
    - Zone-to-driver assignment model
  low_confidence_areas:
    - Deviation detection threshold is unspecified (GPS distance? sequence divergence? time-based?)
    - Multi-driver zone allocation strategy is not defined
    - Failed delivery disposition (retry logic, max attempts) is unspecified
    - Recalculation debouncing/batching strategy needs operational input
    - What constitutes "optimized" is undefined (shortest distance? fewest turns? time-based?)
    - Whether notification window should be configurable (always 3?) or zone-specific
  assumptions:
    - One driver per zone per shift (not explicitly stated — may be wrong for large zones)
    - Vehicle class is determined by the vehicle assigned to the shift, not just driver license
    - "Optimized" means minimizing total route time including stop times
    - Failed delivery is removed from current route (not auto-retried in same shift)
    - Customer notification is a one-time event per delivery attempt, not per recalculation
```

---

## Critical Observations for the Team

**1. The product spec conflates four bounded contexts.** Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Fulfillment, and Customer Notification must be separated. A notification should not be coupled to route calculation internals — it should react to domain events.

**2. Rule FD-1 (vehicle class) is the hardest invariant.** This is not a business preference; it is a legal and safety requirement. It must be enforced at the domain model level, not at the API or UI layer. If the route optimizer can even *see* ineligible deliveries, the design is wrong.

**3. The "next 3 stops" notification is a customer promise, not a status update.** The product team must decide: once a customer is notified, is this a commitment (driver MUST deliver within the window) or a best-effort estimate? This decision fundamentally changes the recalculation algorithm's constraints.

**4. "Deviation" is dangerously underspecified.** A driver taking a parallel street is not the same as a driver skipping a stop. The team needs to define deviation in terms of **stop sequence divergence**, not just GPS drift. GPS-only deviation detection will cause phantom recalculations in dense urban environments.

**5. Multi-driver zones are the hidden complexity bomb.** The spec says "all pending deliveries in their zone" — but what if two drivers share a zone? The delivery allocation strategy (who gets which delivery) is a critical missing business rule that will cause bugs if left to implementation-time decisions.
